county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Guaynabo, established 1769, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Guaynabo, established as a parish around 1769, is part of the San Juan metropolitan area. Parroquia San Pedro Mártir holds records from the late 18th century. Despite modern suburban development, Guaynabo has deep historical roots. Hacienda records at AGPR document sugar cultivation along the Río Bayamón and its tributaries. The municipio's proximity to San Juan means many families appear in both jurisdictions' records. Caparra, the first Spanish settlement in Puerto Rico (1508-1521), is located in Guaynabo — early colonial records at AGPR and the Archivo General de Indias (Seville) document this period. Check notarial protocols for suburban development-era property transactions.

source_profiles.source_name: Guaynabo Catholic Parish Registers (Baptisms, Marriages, Burials)
source_profiles.repository: Parish Archives / FamilySearch / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Guaynabo municipio including baptisms (bautismos), marriages (matrimonios), and burials (defunciones). FamilySearch has digitized microfilm for many Puerto Rico parishes. Coverage begins circa 1769.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish registers are the primary genealogical source for Puerto Rico. Some early volumes deteriorated due to tropical climate. FamilySearch microfilm provides backup copies.

source_profiles.source_name: Guaynabo Civil Registry (Registro Demográfico) — Births, Marriages, Deaths
source_profiles.repository: Registro Demográfico, Guaynabo / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil registry records for Guaynabo including births, marriages, and deaths from 1885. Spanish civil registration began 1885 under Royal Decree. US administration continued the system after 1898.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil registry records generally well-preserved from 1885. Some gaps in early years due to incomplete compliance. Tropical climate and hurricanes have affected some volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Guaynabo Notarial Protocols (Protocolos Notariales)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Notarial protocols for Guaynabo district covering property transfers, testaments, powers of attorney, marriage contracts, slave sales, coartación agreements, and other legal instruments from 1769. Spanish legal tradition required notarized documents for most transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Notarial protocols at AGPR are generally well-preserved. Some volumes from the earliest periods show deterioration. AGPR's climate-controlled storage protects these irreplaceable records.

source_profiles.source_name: Guaynabo Spanish Colonial Census Records (Padrones)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan / Archivo Histórico Nacional, Madrid
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Spanish colonial census records (padrones) for Guaynabo. Include population counts by race, age, occupation, and household. Key padrones: 1765, 1776, 1800, 1812, 1820, 1846, 1860, 1887, 1897. Some padrones list individuals by name; others are statistical summaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survival varies by year. The 1800, 1860, and 1897 padrones are among the most complete. Some early padrones survive only as summaries at the Archivo Histórico Nacional in Madrid.

source_profiles.source_name: Guaynabo Property Registry (Registro de la Propiedad)
source_profiles.repository: Registro de la Propiedad, Guaynabo district
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property registry records for Guaynabo district. The Spanish mortgage law system (Ley Hipotecaria) was established in Puerto Rico in 1870, creating a formal property registration system that continues today. Records document property ownership, transfers, mortgages, and liens.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Property registry records are generally well-preserved from 1870. The formal registration system provides continuous chains of title.

source_profiles.source_name: Guaynabo Municipal Cemetery Records (Cementerio Municipal)
source_profiles.repository: Municipio de Guaynabo / Find A Grave
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Municipal cemetery records for Guaynabo. Municipal cemeteries were established in most Puerto Rico municipios during the 19th century under Spanish health regulations. Records include burial registers, grave assignments, and monument inscriptions. Find A Grave has partial crowdsourced transcriptions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Municipal cemetery records vary in completeness. Earlier burials were in church cemeteries (camposantos). Some municipal cemetery records were damaged by hurricanes.

source_profiles.source_name: Guaynabo Slave Registers and Abolition Records (1872-1873)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Slave registers (registros de esclavos) for Guaynabo including the 1872 census of enslaved persons mandated before the 1873 Abolition Law. Also includes coartación (self-purchase) agreements, liberto (freed persons) records, and slave sale documents in notarial protocols. Essential for tracing African-descended families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: The 1872 slave register is generally well-preserved at AGPR. Earlier slave records are scattered across notarial protocols, church registers, and estate inventories.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Gurabo, established 1815, carved from Caguas. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Gurabo was carved from Caguas in 1815 in the Turabo Valley. Known as 'La Ciudad del Eterno Palmar.' Parroquia San José holds records from the early 19th century. Pre-1815 records are in Caguas parish archives. Sugar and tobacco cultivation dominated — hacienda records at AGPR document both landowners and workers. The Universidad del Turabo (now Universidad Ana G. Méndez) area has modern settlement patterns. Gurabo's position in the fertile Turabo Valley made it agriculturally important — check notarial protocols for sugar estate transactions. Barrios like Celada, Hato Nuevo, Jagüeyes, Mamey, and Navarro each have distinctive settlement records from the colonial era.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Guánica, established 1914, carved from Yauco. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Guánica was carved from Yauco in 1914 on the southwest coast. Historically significant as the landing site for US forces during the 1898 Spanish-American War. Parroquia San Antonio de Padua holds records from 1914 — pre-1914 records are in Yauco's parish. The Guánica Dry Forest (Bosque Seco de Guánica) area has distinct barrio records. Sugar industry records, particularly the Guánica Centrale (Central Guánica), one of the world's largest sugar mills, provide extensive employment and residential data. US military invasion records at NARA document the July 25, 1898 landing. Afro-Puerto Rican sugar worker families are documented in plantation payrolls and civil registry records.

source_profiles.survival_notes: US federal census records for Puerto Rico are generally complete from 1910. The 1950 census is the most recent publicly available.

source_profiles.survival_notes: Spanish colonial military records survive at AGPR and in Spanish archives. US military records at NARA are generally complete from 1898.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Hatillo, established 1823, carved from Arecibo. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hatillo was carved from Arecibo in 1823 on the north coast. Known for the Festival de las Máscaras (December 28). Parroquia Nuestra Señora del Carmen holds records from the 1820s. Pre-1823 records are in Arecibo's extensive parish archives. The name derives from 'hato' (cattle ranch), reflecting the area's pastoral origins before sugar cultivation. Dairy farming remains important — agricultural cooperative records provide modern genealogical data. Co-parent to Camuy (1807), Hatillo records interrelate with several north-coast municipios. Canary Islander (isleño) settlement is documented in colonial passenger records and church registers. Check AGPR for colonial-era cattle brand registrations.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Hormigueros, established 1874, carved from Mayagüez. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hormigueros was carved from Mayagüez in 1874 on the west coast, one of Puerto Rico's smallest municipios. The Basílica Menor Nuestra Señora de la Monserrate is a famous pilgrimage site — its extensive parish records from the 1870s include registers from devotees across the island. Pre-1874 records are in Mayagüez's parish archives. The miracle tradition associated with the shrine means baptism records include families from many different municipios. Coffee and sugar cultivation in surrounding areas generated hacienda records at AGPR. Check Mayagüez's church and notarial records extensively for pre-separation family data. Hormigueros residents frequently appear in Mayagüez and San Germán records.

county_metadata.record_losses: Hurricane María (2017) caused damage to municipal infrastructure in Humacao. Some local government records and documents may have been affected by flooding and wind damage.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Humacao, established 1793, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Humacao, established as a parish around 1793, is the principal city of the eastern coast. Parroquia Dulce Nombre de Jesús holds records from the late 18th century. As parent to Juncos (1797), Las Piedras (1801), Naguabo (1821), and co-parent to Ceiba, Humacao records serve a wide eastern region. Sugar plantations along the coast generated extensive enslaved persons records — the 1872 slave register for Humacao at AGPR is a key source. French, Corsican, and Mallorcan immigration is well-documented. The port of Humacao facilitated trade — customs records at AGPR and NARA provide passenger and cargo data. Universidad de Puerto Rico campus adds modern research resources.

source_profiles.repository: Colección Puertorriqueña, UPR / Biblioteca Nacional / Chronicling America
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historical newspapers published in or covering Humacao. Puerto Rico's newspaper tradition began in the early 19th century. Papers include birth/death/marriage announcements, legal notices, estate settlements, and community news. La Gaceta de Puerto Rico (official gazette) published government notices.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some newspaper runs are incomplete. The Colección Puertorriqueña at UPR Río Piedras has the most extensive holdings. Chronicling America has digitized selected Puerto Rico newspapers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Isabela, established 1819, carved from Aguadilla. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Isabela was carved from Aguadilla in 1819 on the northwest coast. Parroquia San Antonio de Padua holds records from the early 19th century. Pre-1819 records are in Aguadilla and ultimately Aguada. Sugar and later dairy farming dominated — hacienda records at AGPR trace agricultural families. The Pozo de Jacinto sinkhole and Guajataca tunnel area barrios have distinct histories. Isabela's coastline attracted fishing families whose records appear in boat registrations and fishing permits. Canary Islander settlement is documented in colonial records. Check Aguadilla parish baptism registers for pre-1819 family connections. The Río Guajataca valley settlements predate the municipio's formal creation.

source_profiles.source_name: Isabela Catholic Parish Registers (Baptisms, Marriages, Burials)
source_profiles.repository: Parish Archives / FamilySearch / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Isabela municipio including baptisms (bautismos), marriages (matrimonios), and burials (defunciones). FamilySearch has digitized microfilm for many Puerto Rico parishes. Coverage begins circa 1819.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish registers are the primary genealogical source for Puerto Rico. Some early volumes deteriorated due to tropical climate. FamilySearch microfilm provides backup copies.

source_profiles.source_name: Isabela Civil Registry (Registro Demográfico) — Births, Marriages, Deaths
source_profiles.repository: Registro Demográfico, Isabela / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil registry records for Isabela including births, marriages, and deaths from 1885. Spanish civil registration began 1885 under Royal Decree. US administration continued the system after 1898.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil registry records generally well-preserved from 1885. Some gaps in early years due to incomplete compliance. Tropical climate and hurricanes have affected some volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Isabela Notarial Protocols (Protocolos Notariales)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Notarial protocols for Isabela district covering property transfers, testaments, powers of attorney, marriage contracts, slave sales, coartación agreements, and other legal instruments from 1819. Spanish legal tradition required notarized documents for most transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Notarial protocols at AGPR are generally well-preserved. Some volumes from the earliest periods show deterioration. AGPR's climate-controlled storage protects these irreplaceable records.

source_profiles.source_name: Isabela Spanish Colonial Census Records (Padrones)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan / Archivo Histórico Nacional, Madrid
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Spanish colonial census records (padrones) for Isabela. Include population counts by race, age, occupation, and household. Key padrones: 1765, 1776, 1800, 1812, 1820, 1846, 1860, 1887, 1897. Some padrones list individuals by name; others are statistical summaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survival varies by year. The 1800, 1860, and 1897 padrones are among the most complete. Some early padrones survive only as summaries at the Archivo Histórico Nacional in Madrid.

source_profiles.source_name: Isabela Property Registry (Registro de la Propiedad)
source_profiles.repository: Registro de la Propiedad, Isabela district
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property registry records for Isabela district. The Spanish mortgage law system (Ley Hipotecaria) was established in Puerto Rico in 1870, creating a formal property registration system that continues today. Records document property ownership, transfers, mortgages, and liens.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Property registry records are generally well-preserved from 1870. The formal registration system provides continuous chains of title.

source_profiles.source_name: Isabela Municipal Cemetery Records (Cementerio Municipal)
source_profiles.repository: Municipio de Isabela / Find A Grave
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Municipal cemetery records for Isabela. Municipal cemeteries were established in most Puerto Rico municipios during the 19th century under Spanish health regulations. Records include burial registers, grave assignments, and monument inscriptions. Find A Grave has partial crowdsourced transcriptions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Municipal cemetery records vary in completeness. Earlier burials were in church cemeteries (camposantos). Some municipal cemetery records were damaged by hurricanes.

county_metadata.record_losses: Hurricane María (2017) caused damage to municipal infrastructure in Jayuya. Some local government records and documents may have been affected by flooding and wind damage.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Jayuya, established 1911, carved from Utuado. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jayuya was carved from Utuado in 1911 in the heart of the Cordillera Central. Named after the Taíno cacique Hayuya — the Cemi Museum and Taíno petroglyphs at the La Piedra Escrita are nationally significant. Parroquia Nuestra Señora de la Monserrate holds records from 1911 — pre-1911 records are in Utuado. The 1950 Jayuya Uprising (Revolución Nacionalista) resulted in martial law — military and police records at NARA and AGPR document participants. Coffee is the primary agricultural product — coffee hacienda records at AGPR trace highland families. Mountain isolation preserved traditional settlement patterns — barrio-level research is critical for tracing families in this remote area.

source_profiles.source_name: Jayuya US Federal Census Records (1910-1950)
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: US federal census records for Jayuya from 1911. Puerto Rico was included in US census enumerations from 1910. Pre-formation census data for this area is found under parent municipio(s). The 1910-1940 census records are accessible on FamilySearch and Ancestry.
source_profiles.survival_notes: US federal census records for Puerto Rico are generally complete from 1910. The 1950 census is the most recent publicly available.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Juana Díaz, established 1798, carved from Coamo, Ponce. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Juana Díaz was carved from Coamo and Ponce in 1798 on the south-central coast. Known for the Festival de los Santos Reyes (Three Kings festival). Parroquia San Ramón Nonato holds records from the late 18th century. Pre-1798 records split between Coamo (one of PR's oldest parishes) and Ponce. Sugar plantations along the coast generated enslaved persons records — slave registers and manumission documents at AGPR are important. Co-parent to Villalba (1917), so Villalba's pre-1917 records are partly here. The south coast rail line records document transportation workers. Check both Coamo and Ponce church registers for pre-separation families.

source_profiles.source_name: Juana Díaz Slave Registers and Abolition Records (1872-1873)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Slave registers (registros de esclavos) for Juana Díaz including the 1872 census of enslaved persons mandated before the 1873 Abolition Law. Also includes coartación (self-purchase) agreements, liberto (freed persons) records, and slave sale documents in notarial protocols. Essential for tracing African-descended families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: The 1872 slave register is generally well-preserved at AGPR. Earlier slave records are scattered across notarial protocols, church registers, and estate inventories.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Juncos, established 1797, carved from Humacao. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Juncos was carved from Humacao in 1797 in the eastern interior. Known as 'La Ciudad del Valenciano.' Parroquia Nuestra Señora del Carmen holds records from the late 18th century. Pre-1797 records are in Humacao's parish archives. Sugar and tobacco cultivation dominated — hacienda records at AGPR and notarial protocols document landowning families. The Valencian settler tradition is reflected in place names and cultural practices. Juncos sits in the Río Valenciano valley — property records at Registro de la Propiedad track land distribution along the valley. Check civil registry from 1885 for vital records. Barrios like Ceiba Norte, Ceiba Sur, Gurabo Abajo, and Lirios have distinctive records.
